data.title: What impact will Warsh's nomination for Federal Reserve Chairman have on BTC?
data.description:
Shan Ouba, Golden Finance

On January 30, 2026, former US President Trump officially announced on the Truth social media platform that he would nominate Kevin Warsh to be the next Chairman of the Federal Reserve. This news quickly attracted widespread attention in global financial markets and the cryptocurrency field. As the core leader of the Federal Reserve, the policy inclinations of the appointee will directly affect the liquidity of the US dollar and global asset pricing. How does Warsh view BTC? What impact will his appointment have on the crypto market?

I. Who is Kevin Warsh?

Kevin Warsh is not an unfamiliar face in the market.

Public information shows that Warsh served as a member of the White House Council of Economic Advisers during the Bush administration and as a member of the Federal Reserve Board of Governors from 2006 to 2011. He was deeply involved in core work such as financial regulatory reform and monetary policy formulation after the global financial crisis, and has a profound understanding of the operating logic of the US financial system.

After leaving the Federal Reserve, Warsh joined the Hoover Institution as a distinguished visiting scholar and also became a partner at the Duquesne family office. During this time, he frequently participated in financial industry seminars and published policy analyses, paying particular attention to the development of emerging financial technology fields. In addition, he has spoken at numerous cryptocurrency industry events, offering clear views on issues such as the nature and regulation of Bitcoin. His stance is neither aggressively bullish nor blindly rejecting, consistently revolving around "compliance, rationality, and marketization." This aligns closely with the Trump administration's approach of promoting the compliant integration of cryptocurrencies into the US financial system, becoming a significant advantage for his nomination as Chairman of the Federal Reserve. II. How Warsh Views Bitcoin 1. For young people, Bitcoin is becoming the new gold. This is Warsh's most frequently quoted and widely disseminated statement. In a 2021 interview on CNBC's *Squawk Box* discussing the monetary environment and asset allocation, Warsh stated: "If you are under 40, bitcoin is your new gold." This statement wasn't about price prediction, but rather a reflection of generational shifts in asset allocation structures. Warsh's core judgment was that in an environment of prolonged monetary easing and sovereign debt inflation, younger generations tend to choose assets that don't rely on government credit and possess a scarcity narrative. Bitcoin, in this respect, is partially assuming the role that gold once played. It's important to emphasize that he didn't claim that "Bitcoin is replacing gold," but rather that it is "diverting" gold's function in investment portfolios. 2. Bitcoin Makes Sense as Portfolio Parts In the same series of interviews, Warsh explicitly stated: In the current macroeconomic and monetary environment, Bitcoin "makes sense as part of a portfolio." He did not describe Bitcoin as a currency, payment instrument, or institutional asset, but rather discussed it within the framework of "asset allocation." This means that, in Warsh's view, BTC is positioned closer to a highly volatile, non-sovereign store of value asset. Its legitimacy stems from diversifying the risks of the traditional financial system, rather than replacing the existing monetary system. This is a typical example of "limited endorsement" from within the central bank system. 3. Bitcoin is both an important asset and a guardian of monetary policy. In a 2025 interview, Warsh stated that Bitcoin is a highly disruptive new technology, an important asset, and even a "health check" for policymakers. "Whether we are right or wrong, its price speaks volumes, so it's more like an inspector of monetary policy." He further added in numerous public speeches that Bitcoin's price fluctuations not only reflect changes in market supply and demand but also reflect changes in market confidence in the Federal Reserve's monetary policy and the dollar system. Its volatility data can serve as a reference for the Federal Reserve in formulating monetary policy. He opposes excessive intervention by the Federal Reserve in the Bitcoin market and advocates respecting market rules and guiding the industry's healthy development through improved regulation. 4. Bitcoin is not an enemy of the financial system. According to a Politico report (end of 2025), Warsh stated that Bitcoin is not an "enemy" of the financial system and should not be excluded from regulation. Instead, it should be incorporated into the traditional financial system through clear regulatory rules to achieve standardized development. He explicitly supports innovative applications of blockchain technology, favoring the classification of Bitcoin as a legitimate asset class. He believes that through regulated stablecoins, spot ETFs, and other compliant pathways, Bitcoin can realize its market value within a controllable scope, while preventing potential risks such as money laundering and speculation. This stance aligns closely with Trump's "America First" crypto agenda. In conclusion, Warsh is neither a Bitcoin advocate nor a simple denier. To date, Warsh has not made any extreme bullish or bearish statements regarding Bitcoin; all his views revolve around "rational viewing, compliant guidance, and realizing its value." With his nomination as Chairman of the Federal Reserve, his regulatory approach to Bitcoin is likely to gradually take shape, influencing the development of the Bitcoin market in the US and globally. His subsequent statements will continue to be a focus of attention in the financial markets and the crypto industry. This means that Bitcoin is more likely to be seen as a real variable that needs to be priced, regulated, and included in a macro-level perspective, rather than something to be simply ignored or administratively rejected.

data.title: Despite Hyper's claim of "strongest liquidity," its true price still depends on Binance.
data.description:
Hyperliquid founder Jeff recently posted that "Hyperliquid has quietly become the most liquid cryptocurrency price discovery platform globally, with order book depth for BTC perpetual contracts surpassing Binance." At first glance, this seems like a groundbreaking "milestone"—an on-chain derivatives platform surpassing Binance to become the global price discovery center.

However, a basic understanding of the trading market reveals that comparisons like Hyperliquid's are essentially typical "order book performances," not equivalent to real liquidity, much less price discovery power. In fact, this claim could be a misinterpretation of market mechanisms, or even marketing hype.

(Screenshot posted by Jeff: Binance on the left / Hyper on the right) A large number of orders does not equal strong liquidity; Hyper's "depth" is a mechanism illusion. The screenshot shown by Jeff is a typical Orderbook depth comparison. The problem is that Hyperliquid's matching mechanism is not a real-time, high-frequency matching mechanism. A widely discussed fact in the industry is that Hyperliquid matching has a significant latency window (even jokingly referred to by the community as "matching only once every 80 seconds"). This means that user orders are not executed immediately, and a large number of orders accumulate in the order book. The order book looks "thick," but the actual execution efficiency is very low. It's like a farmers' market: Binance is a "real-time exchange," where buy and sell orders are executed instantly. Hyper is more like a "timed auction," where orders pile up waiting for settlement. The result is that Hyper's order depth is more like "stacking depth" than "execution depth." True liquidity must answer one question: Can you execute large trades quickly with extremely low slippage? Binance can, but Hyper may not. Price discovery isn't about order books, it's about "who the global market follows." Jeff says Hyper has become a "global price discovery center." But what are the standards for price discovery? This isn't a screenshot, but rather who determines market prices. Who is the primary reactionary force for global funds? Who has the largest trading volume and open interest? Whose index is cited across the entire industry? The answer is clear: Binance remains the largest pricing anchor for BTC perpetual futures globally. In the real market, CME influences institutional funds, Binance influences global retail investors and market makers, OKX, Bybit, and others follow suit, and the on-chain Perp platform passively follows. Most mainstream market indices (CoinMarketCap, TradingView, Coinalyze) assume that BinancePerp is the core source of BTC price discovery. Even with Hyperliquid's trading volume growth, it falls far short of the level of a "global pricing center." | Trading volume, user base, market maker ecosystem: Hyper cannot compare to Binance. Binance's advantage is not "order screenshots," but its complete financial ecosystem. Binance boasts the world's largest derivatives trading volume; for a long time, Binance's perpetual contract daily trading volume has consistently reached tens to hundreds of billions of dollars. Liquidity remains even during periods of extreme market volatility. Hyperance's size is still in the "new platform growth phase" and cannot support global price discovery. Binance boasts the world's most mature market maker system. Binance's liquidity comes from top-tier market maker institutions such as Jump, Wintertermute, GSR, and Cumberland. These institutions deploy real funds and risk management models on Binance. Hyper's so-called liquidity, however, largely stems from incentive-driven mechanisms, LP rewards, and performative order placement. Liquidity isn't built on screenshots; it's built on long-term capital investment. | The deepest liquidity outside of traditional financial exchanges? More of a gimmick than a fact. Jeff also mentioned that Hyper has become the deepest market for traditional asset perpetual contracts. It sounds impressive, but the question is: where is the real pricing center for TradFi asset derivatives? The S&P 500 is on CME, gold on COMEX, forex on EBS, and US Treasury bonds on the Broker system. Creating an "Apple stock contract" on-chain perpetual contract won't change the TradFi pricing system. Hyper's so-called TradFiPerp (traditional assets) is more of a synthetic asset game within the crypto world. It's a long way from being a houseall of finance (asset center). Binance's advantage lies in real trading, a stable system, and global trust. Hyper can market itself as "upgrading the financial system," but Binance is already doing it, boasting the world's largest user base, strongest risk control system, most mature matching engine, most complete product matrix, and broadest regulatory integration path. Binance doesn't win by order book thickness, but by real trading experience, millisecond-level matching, ability to execute trades even in extreme market conditions, the world's largest liquidity pool, and the lowest slippage—real market depth. Hyper's order book might look more "impressive," but Binance's execution is the "real" result. Hyperliquid's "liquidity myth" is more like marketing hype. Hyperliquid's growth is noteworthy, but Jeff's statement is clearly an exaggeration. The market won't change its pricing center based on a screenshot of an order book. Price discovery belongs to the largest trading volume, the largest liquidity pool, the most mature market-making system, the fastest matching engine, and the broadest global user base. In these metrics, Binance remains ahead. Hyper could argue that it has "thicker order books," but Binance, which Jeff is comparing itself to, is the true price heart of the global crypto market.

data.title: Kevin Warsh, the next chairman of the Federal Reserve
data.description:
According to US media reports, on January 30th, US President Trump met with former Federal Reserve Governor Kevin Warsh at the White House and, in a subsequent phone call, formally asked him if he would be willing to serve as the next Federal Reserve Chairman. Warsh ultimately gave an affirmative answer. This development was quickly reflected in the prediction market. On Polymarket, the contract for "Who will Trump nominate as Fed Chair" is almost unanimously in favor: Kevin Walsh's "Yes" has a probability as high as 94%. Rick Reid has only 4% remaining, with a total transaction volume of $310 million. The market has almost accepted this outcome as a foregone conclusion. Although current Chairman Powell's term doesn't end until May 15th, this early succession arrangement clearly carries a distinct political signal. Why now? On the surface, this appears to be a normal preparation for the transfer of power. But the differences between Trump and himself have long been public: persistently high interest rates, a tight monetary policy pace, and an awkward coordination with fiscal stimulus. In Trump's view, the Fed's willingness to align with the overall economic narrative is more important than simply cutting interest rates. Pre-selecting a successor not only paves the way for the future but also exerts silent pressure on the current administration. Who is Warsh?

Kevin·WarshKevin Warsh),1970born in 1970 into a middle-class family in Albany, New York, and grew up in the suburbs. He repeatedly mentioned that his understanding of the real economy came from places far removed from Wall Street, places devoid of financial jargon, where only business, employment, and cash flow existed. His educational path was extremely elite. He received his undergraduate degree in Public Policy from Stanford University, majoring in Economics and Politics; and his JD from Harvard Law School. After graduating, he didn't pursue a legal career but went directly to Wall Street. From 1995 to 2002, he worked in the mergers and acquisitions department of Morgan Stanley, rising from analyst to executive director, deeply involved in large-scale transactions and developing an extremely keen sense for market signals. In 2002, he joined the Bush administration as Special Assistant to the President for Economic Policy, responsible for capital markets, banking, and insurance regulation. Four years later, at the age of 35, Warsh was nominated to the Federal Reserve Board of Governors, becoming one of the youngest governors in the history of the Federal Reserve at the time. During his tenure from 2006 to 2011, which spanned the global financial crisis, he was both a policymaker and a "firefighter." He played a crucial role in transforming Morgan Stanley and Goldman Sachs into bank holding companies to secure emergency funding from the Federal Reserve, and was often seen as a bridge between the Federal Reserve and Wall Street. During the 2008 financial crisis, he would call Jamie Dimon (CEO of JPMorgan Chase) or Lloyd Blankfein (CEO of Goldman Sachs) to understand the actual market positions and risks. In 2011, he strongly opposed Bernanke's launch of a second round of quantitative easing (QE2), arguing that this "mad printing of money" was mortgaging the future and would exacerbate the wealth gap. When he found he could not persuade others on the committee, he resigned. After leaving office, Warsh entered the academic and private spheres, including serving as a scholar at the Stanford Hoover Institution and a lecturer at business schools, while also joining the boards of companies such as UPS and Coupang. Warsh consistently emphasized rule-based operations, realistic strategies, and a decision-making approach focused on maintaining the Federal Reserve's credibility at crucial moments. His personal background also supported his political and financial networks. In 2002, he married Jane Lauder, an heiress to the Estée Lauder family fortune. This marriage brought him into the core circles of American capital and politics, and established a close relationship with Trump. With his keen market analysis and communication skills, he thrived in elite social circles. He possessed both the acumen of a financier and the eloquence of a "financial diplomat," earning not only the respect of the establishment but also, years later, the ability to gain a following among the unpredictable Trump. In recent years, Warsh's stance has undergone subtle adjustments. While not repudiating his past positions, he has begun to publicly state that failing to cut interest rates at crucial moments could damage the Federal Reserve's credibility and impact economic stability. The market generally interprets this as a realistic adjustment; he is no longer merely a dogmatic hawk, but a pragmatist who emphasizes rules while understanding how to adjust his stance in the face of political realities.

Why did Trump choose him?

Wash's victory in the final shortlist was quite dramatic.

... Just a few days ago, BlackRock executive Rick Rieder was considered the biggest dark horse. While both Rick Rieder and Kevin Hassett have their strengths, for Trump, the logic behind his selection is that trust outweighs political stance. Walsh pinpointed Trump's three core demands during the interview. Warsh explicitly stated that the Federal Reserve "must cut interest rates" and suggested making room for interest rate adjustments by shrinking the Fed's balance sheet. This proposition perfectly aligns with Trump's economic vision of "low interest rates, high growth." At the same time, Warsh has long been a public critic of Powell, accusing the Federal Reserve of systemic errors in its inflation management and emphasizing that his position is consistent with Trump's economic philosophy. What's even more noteworthy is that Walsh excels at using language easily understood by Trump to depict the future, predicting that AI will bring about a "Reagan-era productivity boom," which aligns perfectly with Trump's vision of "making Trump great again." Ultimately, Trump chose "one of his own people he trusted more," rather than simply focusing on candidates with policy leanings. The market's timely reaction to the news of Warsh's selection quickly triggered a chain reaction in the financial markets. Bitcoin plunged more than 5% in the short term, once falling to $85,200. Gold spot prices plummeted by 3.2%, hitting a low of around $5,300 per ounce, while silver prices fell by more than 8%. The US Dollar Index (DXY) rose 0.38% in the short term, briefly touching 104.82, a new high in nearly a week. US stock futures fell across the board, with Nasdaq 100 futures down 0.72%, S&P 500 futures down 0.51%, and Dow Jones futures down 0.42%. U.S. Treasury yields generally rose, with the benchmark 10-year Treasury yield rising by about 7 basis points to around 4.28%; the 2-year yield rose by 5 basis points to... lang="EN-US">3.92%. The market's expectation for the total number of interest rate cuts in 2026 has also been adjusted, from approximately 3.5 times (100 basis points) to 2.3-2.5 times (60-65 basis points). The probability of a 25bp rate cut in three months has decreased from 75% to 58%. This is not simply a game of interest rates, but a contest between financial policy rules and market expectations. Despite Warsh's support for interest rate cuts, which is bearish for the dollar in the short term, the market is also focused on his hawkish stance, tightening of liquidity, and opposition to "money printing." This expectation directly outweighs the short-term benefits of interest rate cuts, putting pressure on Nasdaq futures and other risk assets. In the medium term, Warsh emphasizes "rule-based" operations and opposes excessive intervention. This could provide the market with a more predictable policy framework, but it also means stricter regulation and operations. Bond yields face upward pressure, dollar assets benefit, while equities and emerging market assets face certain challenges. In the long term, the narrative of the Fed's independence will be tested. Although operations may return to rule-based and normalized practices, the White House's increasing influence may cause market confidence in the Fed's policies to fluctuate. Warsh may also cooperate with Trump's fiscal expansion policies within the framework of monetary policy, such as tax cuts, to offset fiscal expansion with tight monetary policy in order to avoid a repeat of high inflation.

Crossroads in the Crypto Market

Wash's attitude towards crypto is somewhat complex; he is neither completely opposed nor fervently supportive.

data.title: The nomination of the Federal Reserve Chair triggered a tsunami in gold stocks and the cryptocurrency market.
data.description:
On Friday, January 30, 2026, global financial markets experienced unprecedented volatility, causing immense anxiety among investors. Particularly concerning was the sharp drop in international spot gold prices from its previous high of nearly $5,600 per ounce, falling to around $5,100 at one point, a single-day decline of 6%-8%, wiping out months of gains. Spot silver fared even more dramatically, plummeting by over 12% at one point. Bitcoin quickly fell from above $89,000, hitting a low of $82,000-$84,000, a drop of approximately 5%-7%, putting pressure on the entire crypto market. The A-share market was not spared either. In the morning session, the precious metals sector led the decline in both Shanghai and Shenzhen markets, with the sector as a whole falling by more than 8%. Many individual stocks approached or directly hit their daily limit down at the open: Sichuan Gold, Zhongjin Gold, Hunan Silver, and Baiyin Nonferrous Metals all hit their daily limit down, while Shandong Gold, Chifeng Gold, and Shengda Resources suffered heavy losses of 8%-10%. According to the latest market data, trading volume increased for many stocks within the sector, indicating significant profit-taking and the release of panic selling. According to multiple media reports, Trump confirmed Thursday evening that the nominee will be announced Friday morning. Market speculation on potential nominees included former Federal Reserve Governor Kevin Warsh (hawkish leanings), Rick Rieder (head of fixed income at BlackRock, dovish with extensive market experience), National Economic Council Director Kevin Hassett, and current Federal Reserve Governor Christopher Waller. Warsh's nomination probability was initially predicted to be high. This directly fueled market concerns about the uncertainty surrounding the interest rate cut path, leading to a short-term rebound in the dollar, upward revisions to real interest rate expectations, and pressure on non-interest-bearing and high-risk assets. As a veteran with over a decade of experience in the crypto market, I've witnessed far too many similar "black swan" moments: from the Christmas sell-off caused by Trump's public criticism of Powell in 2018, to the prolonged correction in gold prices from $2070 to $1620 during the 2022 interest rate hike cycle, and the multiple bear markets in the crypto market with losses exceeding 80%. Today's dramatic shock is not the end of the world, but rather the market's most direct reminder: Overly crowded expectations at high levels will always be corrected; uncertainty is the norm in the financial world. More importantly, it provides us with a valuable opportunity to review the situation, helping us improve our understanding and become more composed. I. A Complete Breakdown of the Event: Data and Logic Correspond One-to-One – Why Did Everything Collapse Simultaneously? Let's use data and facts to reconstruct the entire process step by step and understand the underlying logical connections. The excessive gains in the early stages have accumulated significant vulnerabilities. Entering 2026, precious metals and crypto assets experienced a frenzied surge. International gold prices soared from around $4,300-$4,500 at the beginning of the year to around $5,600, a cumulative increase of over 20%; silver fared even better, repeatedly hitting new historical highs in a short period. The A-share precious metals sector saw astonishing gains over the past year, with some individual stocks rising by over 200%, and most experiencing gains of over 80% in the past month. Bitcoin also rebounded from its lows at the end of 2025, briefly approaching $90,000 at the beginning of 2026. Historical data tells us that when an asset's short-term gains exceed 50%-100%, profit-taking and crowded trades accumulate significant risks. Once an event is triggered, a stampede-like sell-off is almost inevitable. This is highly similar to the 20% pullback in gold prices at the beginning of the Fed's rate hikes in 2022. The uncertainty surrounding Trump's nomination became the direct trigger. Current Chairman Powell's term ends in May 2026, and Trump has repeatedly expressed public dissatisfaction with the "insufficient力度" of rate cuts. The market had previously over-bet on "Trump's rise to power inevitably bringing a large-scale easing and low-interest-rate environment," which fueled a frenzy in non-interest-bearing assets. However, Trump's statement on Thursday night that "the nominee will be announced tomorrow morning" instantly changed the game. The candidate list included both doves like Rieder and hawks like Warsh, raising market concerns about increased Fed independence and a slower rate-cutting path. The result: the dollar index rebounded in the short term, and the expected real yield on 10-year US Treasury bonds was revised upward. Gold, as a typical non-interest-bearing asset, was the first to be impacted—a flash crash began late Thursday night and accelerated in early Friday trading. Silver, with its stronger industrial properties, saw a larger drop. Risk spreads rapidly: from precious metals to cryptocurrencies, and then to global stock markets. Bitcoin is often considered "digital gold," but its high beta nature makes it more susceptible to amplified volatility during periods of tightening liquidity. Starting Thursday evening, Bitcoin quickly fell from a high of $89,000, reaching a low of around $82,000 in early Friday trading, a single-day drop of over 5%. This is related to the simultaneous pressure on Nasdaq tech stocks—heavyweight stocks like Microsoft dragged down US stocks, global risk appetite declined, and deleveraging across multiple assets was triggered. The collective plunge in the A-share precious metals sector is a direct reflection of international price transmission: international gold and silver prices plummeted overnight, and domestic futures and stocks followed suit in the morning, with a wave of limit-down stocks appearing at the open.

  • In-depth background: Macroeconomic expectations are quietly being revisedThe Fed kept interest rates unchanged at its January meeting, and Powell's speech did not specify a timetable for a significant rate cut; coupled with the possibility that Trump's tariff policies may push up inflation expectations, concerns about "high interest rates lasting longer" have resurfaced. Data shows that although the central bank's gold purchases have been stable recently, investors are showing strong profit-taking sentiment. This adjustment is a typical product of a reversal in liquidity expectations, which is similar to the multiple upheavals during the Fed chairman transition period in 2018-2019.

    • II. Core insights from veteran financial professionals: Volatility is the breath of the market, maturity lies in learning from it

    The market is never a straight road, but a mountain road full of ups and downs. Every major shock is a test of our understanding. So-called "safe-haven assets" also exhibit clear cyclicality. Gold shines brightly in environments of high inflation, geopolitical conflict, and low interest rates, but it also experiences significant pullbacks when real interest rates rise and profits are realized. Historical data shows that gold fell by over 20% during the 2022 interest rate hike cycle, and nearly 30% during the "taper tantrum" of 2013. Bitcoin is even more volatile, often experiencing 70%-80% pullbacks during bear markets. The lesson is that no single asset is a permanent safe haven; they all have their own sensitive macroeconomic triggers. Policy uncertainty is the biggest variable, but also a sign of growth. Trump's style has always been unpredictable; the market previously over-interpreted his dovish tendencies, and today is a time for correction. Similar to 2018 when Trump's tweet criticizing Powell caused a single-day plunge of over 10% in US stocks. Expert thinking: Uncertainty is inevitable; instead of trying to predict it accurately, learn to respect it and maintain an open mind. Emotions amplify everything; greed at high levels often comes at a price. When prices rise too quickly in the early stages, market sentiment easily shifts from rationality to frenzy. Today's sharp correction is a natural balancing of this sentiment. Data shows that funds that chase high prices often bear the greatest pressure during pullbacks. III. A Rational Approach to Market Volatility: Emotional Management and General Risk Aversion Thinking Faced with such dramatic market fluctuations, the most important thing is to manage your emotions and remain calm. Below are some general experiences summarized by veteran financial professionals, for reference only, to help you find your inner anchor amidst uncertainty. First, take a deep breath and control your impulses: During a market crash, the most common mistake is panic selling or blindly buying the dip. Stop first and review your focus: is it short-term volatility or long-term logic? History has repeatedly proven that emotional decisions often lead to regret. Focus on the big picture, not a single event: Track key indicators such as real interest rates (US Treasury yields minus inflation expectations), the dollar's trajectory, and geopolitical dynamics. These are the true anchors for precious metals and risk assets. Today's correction is essentially a revision of expectations, not a collapse of fundamentals. Think about diversification and balance: Different assets perform differently in different environments. Bonds are relatively stable when interest rates rise, precious metals have hedging value during periods of inflation, technology growth stocks are highly resilient during periods of easing, and crypto assets represent the digital trend. Understanding their complementarity helps build a more comprehensive perspective. Maintain a flexible mindset and accept volatility as the norm: The market will always have uncertainties. True composure comes from accepting that 20%-50% pullbacks are normal, rather than viewing them as a disaster. In the long run, adhering to rationality often allows one to navigate through cycles. Learn more from history, chase fewer hot topics: Reviewing similar past events (such as the 2018 Fed crisis and the 2022 interest rate hike bear market) reveals that each correction breeds new opportunities. Accumulated knowledge is the best emotional buffer. IV. In Conclusion: Every Major Shock is a Step Towards Greater Maturity Following Trump's nomination today, regardless of whether the outcome leans dovish or hawkish, short-term volatility may continue. But remember: The market always favors those who remain calm and continue learning. Today's volatility is not the end, but a wake-up call and a valuable lesson. It reminds us that the financial world is full of uncertainty, but it is precisely because of this that there are infinite possibilities. Take a deep breath, look rationally, and adopt a long-term perspective. We will all become wiser and more composed through each fluctuation. Let's embrace the next, more stable phase together.

data.title: The black and white of cryptocurrency criminal cases: What crime should investigators be charged with for "stealing cryptocurrency"?
data.description: There's a long-standing saying about how investigative agencies handle cryptocurrency-related criminal cases: "Those who haven't handled it before avoid it, but those who have handled it once are eager to handle it..." Jokes aside, the various chaotic phenomena in cryptocurrency cases are a real phenomenon. For example, the recent case of a Shenzhen technician stealing approximately 180 BTC from an overseas online gambling platform has sparked considerable controversy. In this case, the suspect was detained by police in two different locations, and rumors circulated that a large amount of cryptocurrency was confiscated before the conviction, and then the suspect was quickly released on bail. These rumors, amplified by numerous social media posts, have once again touched a nerve with the public: Did the police in both locations have jurisdiction? Did the gambling platform actually exist at that specific time? Was the swift confiscation and liquidation of the cryptocurrency compliant with regulations? What was the suspect's motivation behind the repeated changes in charges during the investigation? Opinions are divided. Today, the Sa Jie team won't talk about "deep-sea fishing," but rather a topic generally considered more "taboo"—how can investigators ensure the integrity of their official conduct during investigations? I. Investigators using the opportunity of investigations to steal cryptocurrency is a long-standing issue; what crime should they be charged with? "What has been will be again, what has been done will be done again." This statement was later popularized by the philosopher Hegel as: "There is nothing new under the sun." Yes, investigators using the opportunity of investigations to steal cryptocurrency is a long-standing issue. The Hunan Provincial Higher People's Court's case No. (2022) Xiang Xing Zhong 209 revealed such a "big rat" case. (I) A Hunan Investigator's Coin Theft Case Yan Moumou was originally a police officer. In early 2019, while working at a police station, he was seconded to investigate a case involving a certain organization and its leader engaging in pyramid schemes. In early May 2019, while cleaning up the assets involved in the case, Yan Moumou discovered that a certain online application app was installed on the phone of suspect Zhang Moumou. Yan Moumou then used this phone to obtain the login verification code for the online platform and, based on his knowledge of Zhang Moumou's password settings, opened Zhang Moumou's online account. Subsequently, Yan downloaded a certain online app on his mobile phone and opened a trading account and cryptocurrency wallet. On May 15th, Yan directly transferred virtual digital currency worth approximately 15.48 million yuan from Zhang's account to his own account, making a profit of 71,161.47 yuan in four OTC transactions. Unexpectedly, shortly after Yan sold this batch of coins, the task force hired a third-party technology company to clean up the virtual digital currency accounts of Zhang, Liao, and other suspects involved in a pyramid scheme (presumably to facilitate subsequent price appraisal and audit reports). Fearing discovery of his theft, Yan transferred the coins already transferred to his account but not yet sold back to Zhang's account. In May 2019, while cleaning up the seized assets, Yan also discovered screenshots of private keys on Liao's phone. Yan transferred 22,520,075 cryptocurrencies, worth approximately 500,000 yuan at the time, from Liao's account to his own wallet, profiting about 260,000 yuan after multiple OTC transactions. In late May, Yan changed Liao's account password using Liao's phone and then transferred the account to his own phone. From June 30 to October 6 of the same year, Yan used cryptocurrencies worth approximately 5 million yuan in Liao's account to trade futures contracts. After the case was uncovered, Yan cooperated with the investigation authorities to liquidate Liao's account. (II) What crime should be charged? 1. Yan Moumou is guilty of embezzlement. Initially, after Yan Moumou's case came to light, the public security organs initiated an investigation on suspicion of "concealing or covering up the proceeds of crime or the proceeds of crime." Later, during the prosecution review stage, the charge was changed to "theft," leading to his arrest and prosecution. During the first instance trial, Yan Moumou's defense attorney argued that there were significant procedural flaws in the case. Considering Yan Moumou's public office status, the investigating authority should have been the supervisory commission, not the public security organs; therefore, some of the evidence on file was obtained illegally and should be excluded. The court of first instance held that the facts alleged by the prosecution were clear, and the evidence was conclusive and sufficient, but the charge was inappropriate. After the second hearing in this case, the Yueyang Intermediate People's Court of Hunan Province found Yan guilty of embezzlement and sentenced him to eleven years in prison, a fine of 500,000 yuan, and deprivation of his political rights for two years. Yan appealed, but the Hunan Provincial Higher People's Court rejected his appeal. 2. The Dispute in This Case Regarding the conviction in this case, the Sa Jie team has no objection. Embezzlement is a crime of status. As a police officer, Yan is a state employee. He abused his position in investigating major criminal cases and managing the inventory of involved assets to illegally transfer the suspect's cryptocurrency to his own name, selling some for profit and using the rest for investment. His actions meet the relevant provisions of Article 382 of the Criminal Law of the People's Republic of my country regarding "embezzlement." The main points of contention in this case are twofold: First, given that Yan committed a crime while in office, is it in accordance with Chinese law for the public security organs to investigate him? Second, does the virtual currency illegally obtained by Yan constitute "public property" as required for the crime of embezzlement? Regarding the first point, the issue of the investigating authority: This case was initiated and investigated as a crime of concealing or covering up the proceeds of crime. Although the choice of charge did not take into account the fact that Yan committed a crime by abusing his position, according to the Criminal Procedure Law and the Regulations on the Division of Jurisdiction over Criminal Cases issued by the Ministry of Public Security, the public security organs in the place where the crime was committed have jurisdiction over cases of concealing or covering up the proceeds of crime. In this case, after the initial investigation and approval of arrest by the procuratorate, the procuratorate, after review, only changed the charge to theft before approving the arrest, without raising any objection to the jurisdiction of the investigating authorities. Therefore, a special situation arises: although the public security organs and the procuratorate improperly characterized the case, the public security organs, having jurisdiction over both concealment of crimes and theft, did not violate the law by continuing the investigation. Secondly, regarding whether cryptocurrency constitutes "public property" as required by the crime of embezzlement, some argue that these cryptocurrencies were not initially discovered or clearly identified as involved property during the public security investigation. Although the suspect's mobile phone was seized, the cryptocurrency was not on the phone. The Xiao Sa legal team believes that, according to Article 91, Paragraph 2 of my country's Criminal Law, "public property" is defined as: "Private property under the management, use, or transportation of state organs, state-owned companies, enterprises, collective enterprises, and people's organizations shall be considered public property." It is a certain fact that the suspect's mobile phone has been lawfully seized by the public security organs. It has also been established that the suspect converted a large amount of criminal proceeds into cryptocurrency during the commission of the crime of organizing and leading criminal activities. In other words, the public security organs only lacked precise knowledge of the assets involved, but had already taken effective investigation and control measures on the relevant assets, pending a subsequent professional investigation by a third-party agency. Therefore, the virtual currency illegally obtained by the suspect Yan in this case is actually private property already under the management of the public security organs and should be considered public property according to law. II. How to Prevent Investigators from Using Case-Handling as an Opportunity to "Steal Cryptocurrencies"? Integrity and honesty are among the most important qualities of public officials, one of the greatest expectations the public has of them, and a crucial source of credibility for state power organs. The Sa Jie team believes that to prevent investigators from using case-handling as an opportunity to "steal cryptocurrencies," the following points are crucial: First, comprehensive, complete, and professional education on blockchain and encrypted assets should be provided to investigators working on the front lines. Based on the experience of the "Sister Sa" team, many frontline investigators still have a "chaotic" understanding of crypto assets. They mistakenly believe that crypto assets (especially BTC and ETH) are "high-value," "hidden," "illegal," and "shady" black market assets, but they know very little about the underlying blockchain technology, the subcategories of crypto assets, and existing fund tracing technologies. This leads many frontline investigators to have ulterior motives after initially understanding the value of crypto assets, harboring the illusion that "stealing a little bit won't get them into trouble." Little do they know that even the smallest piece of evidence cannot escape the "memory" of the blockchain, and can still be used to hold people accountable even years later. Secondly, it is still necessary to ensure the welfare and protection of frontline investigators. Professor Lü Dewen of Huazhong University of Science and Technology once conducted a study on the deep-seated reasons for profit-driven law enforcement and carried out long-term research in a grassroots public security organ in central my country. His research article, "How Does 'Profit-Driven Law Enforcement' by Police Occur?", discusses this issue. The article states, "The Public Security Bureau is so poor that it has to resort to 'revenue generation' through law enforcement to solve its operational problems." Furthermore, manpower shortages and the fact that many frontline investigators are working excessively and fatigued, with their income disproportionate to their workload, have persisted for years. Therefore, ensuring adequate welfare and benefits is a crucial measure to prevent investigators from using their positions to "steal cryptocurrency." In conclusion, it's a familiar point: relevant departments should promptly issue specific regulations for the management and disposal of cryptocurrency involved in cases, clarifying the operational procedures, responsible parties, and oversight mechanisms at each stage to strengthen supervision and control over the entire process of handling such cryptocurrency. Simultaneously, introducing third-party professional institutions to participate in the management, disposal, and auditing of cryptocurrency involved in cases is also important. The involvement of third-party organizations not only enhances the professionalism and efficiency of the process, but their independent status also provides effective external oversight of investigative agencies, reducing opportunities for opaque internal operations.

data.title: Warsh: I heard it's all my fault?
data.description:
Market fluctuations are never short of explanations, and most are just hindsight comforts. The morning's market volatility was explained as being related to Warsh's impending nomination as Federal Reserve Chairman. It's foreseeable that a deluge of quick, superficial commentary on Warsh will soon follow. Below is a summary of Warsh's own remarks, for readers who don't want secondhand accounts. I. 2006: Onboarding Confirmation Hearing I hope my previous experience on Wall Street, especially my nearly seven years at Morgan Stanley, will be helpful to the Federal Reserve's deliberations and communications. II. 2007: On the Eve of the Crisis March 2007 "Market Liquidity" Speech The benefits of ample liquidity are substantial… but historical experience shows that markets can become severely illiquid due to investor risk aversion and increased uncertainty. While policymakers and market participants are certain that these events will occur, they must remain humble in predicting the timing, scope, and duration of these periods of financial distress. (September 2007 Albany University Speech) Regarding my upbringing: I learned most of what it takes to understand the real economy during my first eighteen years here (upstate New York).

November 2007 "The End of History?" speech

If the end of economic history is imminent, what will it look like? You will almost certainly find strong, synchronized global economic growth; good inflation data and anchored inflation expectations; low global risk premiums and low term premiums; low volatility in asset markets…

In my view, the recent financial turmoil should not be seen as a historical accident. Rather, it is history's latest reminder to policymakers and market participants—we should remain humble in our beliefs and cautious in our actions. Market confidence ultimately breeds complacency.

III. 2008: The Financial Crisis March 18, 2008 FOMC Meeting (Before Bear Stearns' bailout) I believe the most fundamental issue is that the business model of investment banks has been threatened, and I doubt that the existing business model can survive this period. March 2008 FOMC Meeting On Inflation: Regarding inflation, there is little reason to believe that inflation will decline. This may be the most severe test for central banks in a generation. The Federal Reserve is not omniscient. Our tools are not unique or perfect in ensuring that yesterday's mistakes will not be repeated. Nor can we guarantee that our policy responses alone will put the economy on a stable and clear path to unparalleled prosperity. We face serious risks if we overestimate our knowledge or over-intervene. Just look at those recent financial gurus who thought they had controllable risks and exponential returns. Milton Friedman warned us long ago, and Edmund Phelps recently reminded us of the "consequences of conceit." Even if the economy weakens further, we should tend to resist the anticipated, reflexive calls for another round of hammering. Policy actions should reinforce the idea among stakeholders that further hammering is necessary, but it needs to be done by financial institutions themselves. In my view, the proper functioning of credit markets and their support for economic growth can only be restored when private financial entities resume their role as the primary source of market liquidity. September 2008 FOMC Meeting (after the Lehman Brothers collapse) I remain unprepared to abandon my concerns about inflation. Bernanke's reaction (venting in an email to Don Kohn the next day): I found myself reassuring those with irrational views—those who believed that even with the economy and financial system in danger and inflation/commodity pressures seemingly easing, we should tighten policy. This domestic housing-centric diagnosis has also undergone a natural test over the past few months. In US financial institutions, concerns about asset quality are no longer limited to the mortgage sector. IV. 2009: After the Crisis Speech at "The Panic of 2008" on April 6, 2009 I should point out that the encouraging news is that the panic will end. When the strong live in a neighborhood that is being destroyed, they find it difficult to prosper, let alone survive. When panic persists and long-held beliefs lose followers, markets tend to react indiscriminately. June 2009 "Defining Deviancy" speech: On the definition of "normal": The title itself is significant—"Definition Bias"—suggesting that the crisis has altered people's understanding of "normal." September 25, 2009 "Longer Days, Fewer Weekends" speech: If policymakers insist on waiting for a clear and substantial return to normalcy in actual economic activity…they will almost certainly wait far too long. There is a risk, the size of which is still debated, that unusually high levels of reserves, coupled with the large amount of liquid assets in the banking system, could trigger an unexpected and excessive surge in lending. Two years after the outbreak of the financial crisis—about a year after the darkest days of the 2008 panic—now is not the time to declare victory, nor is it almost the time to award medals. I believe that the final record of economic history will determine that winning the battle against the 2008 panic was a necessary but not sufficient condition for winning peace and ensuring a solid foundation for economic prosperity. Some say that "at all costs" is the motto that marks the past year's battle. But it cannot be an asymmetrical mantra—used only during periods of deep economic and financial distress and discarded when the cycle turns. V. 2010: The QE2 Debate February 3, 2010 Speech "Regulation and Its Discontents" The panic of 2008, which exacerbated the recession, was the result of numerous flawed private and public practices, with regulatory errors being only one part of it. Reforms must encourage strong competition. Market entry and exit can be more effective means of developing a stronger, more resilient financial system. The "too big to fail" problem may be alleviated if smaller, more dynamic companies take market share from less agile incumbents. March 26, 2010, "An Ode to Independence" Speech: There is no such thing as 'somewhat independent' or 'somewhat credible.' Therefore, central bankers must remain vigilant, especially during periods of fiscal expansion. Even if central banks can do it, it doesn't mean they should. The 2008 panic is just the beginning. The Fed's future actions—especially while economic conditions remain unfavorable—should strongly demonstrate its commitment to its mission. Central bankers are granted a revocable privilege. Therefore, the Fed policymakers' declarations of independence are encouraging. But independence is something we need to prove, not primarily declare. At the November 2010 FOMC meeting (QE2 vote), when the unemployment rate was nearing 10%, he said to Bernanke: "If I were sitting in your chair, I wouldn't lead the committee in this direction; frankly, if I were sitting in the majority of this room, I would also vote against it. I think we're taking on burdens that should be borne by others—especially growth and employment targets—instead of letting them fall where they should be." We are too quick to accept dangerous policies that others have long established; we should place the burden on them. November 8, 2010, "Rejecting the Requiem" speech (SIFMA Annual Meeting) One week after voting in favor of QE2: Opening statement: They call it the "new normal." I call it the new depression. I reject that view. I believe this emerging mentality is dangerous, defeatist, and refuted by America's own extraordinary economic history. The pessimistic economic story being told is not inevitable. Our citizens are not innocent victims of some unavoidable fate. The current below-standard growth and high unemployment are real, but they don't have to continue. We shouldn't lower our expectations. We should improve our policies. Regarding short-termism: Chronic short-termism in economic policy implementation has largely led us to this dangerous situation. Regarding fiscal stimulus: These programs may indeed boost GDP for a quarter or two, but that hardly explains their full effect. In my view, these stimulus programs have done little to put the economy on a stronger, more sustainable path. Sound fiscal policy must do more than just reintroduce consumers to old, bad habits. Regarding the supply side: If left untreated, cyclical issues will become structural. Persistent weakness in the labor market will effectively and permanently displace more workers from the labor market. Regarding the role of the Federal Reserve: The Federal Reserve is not a repair shop for broken fiscal, trade, or regulatory policies. Given the causes of our illness, additional monetary policy measures are at best a poor substitute for stronger growth policies. If the Fed promises too much or fails to deliver, it may lose its hard-won credibility—and monetary policy may lose its considerable influence. We should be wary of drawing inappropriate lessons from the crisis… The power of lender of last resort cannot easily translate into the power of the primary fighter. Regarding the risks of QE2: Expanding the Federal Reserve's balance sheet is not a free option. Significant risks exist, requiring close monitoring by the FOMC. The Treasury market is unique. It plays a unique role in the global financial system. It is a consequence of the dollar's role as the world's reserve currency. The price of Treasury securities—the risk-free rate—is the basis for calculating the prices of almost all assets in the world. As the Federal Reserve's balance sheet expands, it increasingly acts as a price setter rather than a price taker in the Treasury market. There is also a more subtle risk in the Federal Reserve's increased involvement in the long-term Treasury market—blurring price signals about total U.S. debt. Extraordinary measures often lead to extraordinary countermeasures. Second-order effects may produce first-order consequences. VI. 2011: Resignation Letter to President Obama dated February 10, 2011. No specific reasons were given, and his term was originally scheduled to end on January 31, 2018. Larry Kudlow's assessment (CNBC): "a hard money hawk" (Central News Agency) VII. After leaving the Fed (2011-2025) 2014 "Warsh Review" (Bank of England Review) Core argument: More transparency is not always better. After the Federal Reserve released the FOMC minutes, "free and fluent discussion was replaced by pre-prepared, canned speeches." A 2015 CNBC interview on market pricing distortion stated: "The financial markets and the Treasury market tell us almost nothing about the state of the economy because central banks are influencing those prices with every word, every minute speech by every regional Fed president. We would be much better off letting the markets set the prices, instead of having a group of government officials guiding them seven years into the U.S. economic recovery." This, in my view, is quite inconsistent with the original intent of QE. Interest rates should be set by the financial markets, but when the Federal Reserve and other central banks around the world become the first and last buyers, interest rates are not set by the financial markets. October 26, 2015, *Wall Street Journal* column (co-authored with Michael Spence) We believe that QE has shifted capital from the domestic real economy to domestic and foreign financial assets. In this environment, it is difficult to criticize companies that choose "shareholder-friendly" stock buybacks instead of investing in new factories. However, public policy should not favor investment in paper assets over investment in the real economy. The central bank has been quite successful in stimulating investors in financial markets to take risks. Clearly, one reason market participants believe the central bank uses QE is to set a floor for financial asset prices. QE reduces volatility in financial markets, not in the real economy. In fact, as in 2007, when market volatility indicators are at their lowest, actual macroeconomic risks may be at their highest. (August 24, 2016, Wall Street Journal column) The implementation of monetary policy in recent years has been seriously flawed… A robust reform agenda requires a more rigorous review of recent policy choices and significant changes to the Federal Reserve’s tools, strategies, communications, and governance. Two major obstacles must be overcome: groupthink within the academic economics guild and the central bankers' reluctance to relinquish their newfound power. The Federal Reserve often treats financial markets as a wild beast to be tamed, a cub to be pampered, or a market to be manipulated. It appears enslaved by the financial markets, and the financial markets by the Federal Reserve, but only one side has the final say. A simple yet unsettling fact: more than half of the S&P 500's value growth since the beginning of 2008 has occurred on the day the Federal Open Market Committee makes its decisions. Groupthink continues to attract followers, even as its success becomes increasingly difficult to find. The Federal Reserve tightened its grip when it should have been open to new data sources, new analytical methods, new economic models, new communication strategies, and new policy paradigms. Regarding the overreach of functions: Real reform should reverse the trend that has turned the Federal Reserve into a generic government agency. Many members of the Federal Reserve believe that central bankers—nonpartisan, highly respected experts—are particularly well-suited to expanding their policy authority. A 2017 Wall Street Journal column suggested lowering the inflation target from 2% to a 1%-2% range: When broader trends begin to shift—such as in the labor market or output—the Federal Reserve should consider new leading signals. It proposed replacing "data dependence" with "trend dependence."

A retrospective on QE with Bernanke in 2018

My primary concern about continued QE, both in the past and present, concerns capital misallocation in the economy and responsibility misallocation in government.

Misallocations rarely operate in their own name. They choose other names to hide. They often linger in the public eye for years, until they emerge with force at the most ominous moment, causing unexpected damage to the economy.

Anecdotes about Volcker (recounted multiple times by Warsh) Before being nominated as a Federal Reserve Governor, he visited Volcker: He said, "Kevin, there's something I need to tell you. The job you're about to sign is actually very simple. It really only requires two things." (Warsh said he took out a pen and notebook to write it down—but never revealed what Volcker specifically said.) VIII. 2025: Returning to the Public Eye April 25, 2025 "Central Banking at a Crossroads" Speech (G30/IMF) Key Paragraphs of the Complete Speech: Opening: I can't think of a moment more economically important since 1980 than now… Some might argue that the biggest threat to our economy comes from outsiders trying to change the status quo… I disagree… I believe the main risk comes from the choices made within the four walls of our most important economic institutions. Regarding institutional trust: To be trusted, economic institutions must be trustworthy. To be trustworthy, they must prove their competence. Economic institutions must also maintain epistemological humility—that is, acknowledge that knowledge, even great knowledge, has its limitations. Regarding the crisis period: Nothing was easy during that time. We made the right decisions and we made mistakes. Inevitably, the most difficult issue then—and still the most prominent issue now—was the role and responsibility of the Federal Reserve. Quoting Volcker's warning: After a particularly tense weekend that led to unconventional policy support, former Chairman Paul Volcker commented: "The Federal Reserve has reached the very edge of its legitimate and implicit powers, exceeding certain long-embedded central bank principles and practices. We take this as a high and tight warning ball from a powerful institutional ally to its successor." But Volcker's warnings were largely ignored, even to this day. Core criticism: In my view, the overreaching of boundaries on various occasions and for various reasons has led to systemic errors in the implementation of macroeconomic policy. The Federal Reserve is more like a general government agency than a central bank in the narrow sense. Institutional drift occurred simultaneously with the Fed's failure to fulfill a core part of its statutory mandate—price stability. It also led to an explosion in federal spending. The Fed's excessive role and poor performance undermine the important and valuable argument of monetary policy independence. Regarding "fast-food" policy: The frequent changes in the Federal Reserve's indicators—including its claimed preferred measure of inflation—undermine the central bank's esteemed position. I believe the Fed's current "data-dependent" policy has little practical value. We shouldn't pay too much attention to the two decimal places on the latest government data. Holding our breath for lagging data from outdated national accounts—which undergoes significant subsequent revisions—is evidence of false precision and analytical complacency. Recent forecasts are another distracting obsession for the Fed. Economists are not immune to human weaknesses either. Once policymakers reveal their economic projections, they may become prisoners of their own words. Forward guidance—a tool touted during the financial crisis—is virtually useless in normal times. It's tempting to leverage markets with a rolling Fed mantra, but it doesn't help. Central banks should find new comfort in working without applause or breathless spectators. Regarding fiscal dominance: I find it hard to absolve the Fed of responsibility for the nation's fiscal extravagance. Years ago, during difficult times, Fed leaders encouraged government spending. But during periods of sustained growth and full employment, they failed to call for fiscal discipline. Since 2008, the Fed has been the most important buyer of U.S. Treasury bonds—and other U.S. government-backed debt. The Federal Reserve's $7 trillion balance sheet is almost an order of magnitude larger than when I joined. Regarding QE: I strongly support this crisis-time innovation, both in the past and now. But when the crisis ended, the Fed never stopped. In the summer and fall of 2010—a period of strong growth and financial stability—I was deeply concerned that the decision to buy more Treasury bonds would embroil the Fed in the messy politics of fiscal policy. QE2 was announced. I disagreed with the decision and resigned from the Fed shortly afterward. QE—which has had its ups and downs in the 2010s—has become a virtually permanent feature of central bank power and policy. Fiscal policymakers—that is, elected members of Congress—financed that knowing the government's borrowing costs would be subsidized by the central bank, making appropriations much easier. Regarding Monetary Dominance vs. Fiscal Dominance: Fiscal dominance—where national debt constrains monetary policymakers—has long been considered by economists as a possible end state. My view is that monetary dominance—where the central bank becomes the ultimate arbiter of fiscal policy—is a clearer and more realistic danger. Regarding the "Economic Imprinting" Theory: I propose an "economic imprinting" theory, which posits that policy choices made in previous periods make the economy more vulnerable to shocks and less able to organically adjust. Each time the Federal Reserve jumps into action, the scale and scope of its actions expand further, encroaching on other areas of the macroeconomy. More debt accumulation…more capital mismatch…more institutional boundaries being crossed…the risk of future shocks is amplified…the Fed is forced to take more aggressive action next time. Simply put, path dependence is driving policy. We need to be careful it doesn't go astray. Regarding climate and diversity: Climate change and diversity are politicized issues. People of conscience have their own opinions and motivations. The task of elected officials is to assess data, synthesize opinions, formulate policies, and authorize executive agencies when necessary. However, the Fed has neither the expertise nor the privilege to make political judgments in these areas. The Fed's mandate is not to advise, nor to serve as a springboard. Citing Supreme Court precedent: As Supreme Court precedent clearly points out, Congress is not in a position to hide an elephant in a mouse hole. Regarding inflation: The intellectual errors that lead to large inflation include some combination of the following: central banks begin to believe that their price stability goals are largely self-enforcing… those large black-box DSGE models are rooted in reality… monetary policy is unrelated to money… central banks are bystanders of forces outside their control… Putin's rise and the pandemic should be held responsible for inflation, not the surge in government spending and money printing. Stable prices are the main attraction for the Federal Reserve. Like in a movie, it's a protection for the protagonist who dares to challenge. However, the Fed's shifting scope of authority and grand ambitions expand its exposure, making it more vulnerable. Regarding independence: Central bank independence is cited more often than defined. Independence itself is not a policy goal. It is a means to achieve certain important and specific policy outcomes. Whenever Fed policies are criticized, "independence" is reflexively invoked. Congress has granted the Fed important functions, such as bank supervision. I don't believe the Fed should receive any special respect in bank supervision policy. I firmly believe that operational independence of monetary policy is a wise political and economic decision. I believe the Fed's independence depends primarily on the Fed itself. This does not mean that central banks should be treated like pampered princes. When monetary policy fails, the Federal Reserve should be subject to serious scrutiny and strong oversight, and when they err, they should be reprimanded. Regarding the history of the Federal Reserve: The advantages of a narrow central bank are not merely tradition. Our constitutional republic accepts an independent central bank, provided it closely adheres to the responsibilities assigned by Congress and successfully performs its duties. After all, our central bank is the third experiment…not because of the success of its predecessors, but because of their failures. We should remember that the apparent preference of a political system is a deep aversion to inflation—and equally to bailouts and the seizure of power. Core Governance Principles: The governance objective is clear: to ensure the central bank's safety against democracy, not the other way around. Conclusion: The Federal Reserve's current wounds are largely self-inflicted; its protagonist aura is showing signs of erosion. A strategic reset is necessary to mitigate credibility losses, shifting status, and most importantly—worse economic outcomes for our fellow citizens. Central bankers are trained to be cautious in their criticism, lest the sunlight expose their magic. However, a greater risk lies with the wizard's apprentice: abusing magical power to create trouble. We shouldn't worry about violating piety, and we should be prepared to endure periodic frowns of discontent. Now is the time for us to reclaim intellectual freedom and get policy back on track. The legitimacy requirements for central banks are this, and cannot be less. May 28, 2025, Hoover Institution Uncommon Knowledge Interview. You print a trillion here, you print a trillion there. That will catch up with you, Peter. When the Federal Reserve prints trillions of dollars, especially in good times, it changes everything. It's almost sending a signal to the rest of Congress: We're doing this, and so can you. Regarding QE1 in favor but subsequent opposition: I supported it, and many of my colleagues and I supported it, based on the view that we should put these very dangerous, risky things back behind a glass dome until another crisis arrives. We never really did that. So the story you're going to tell about subsequent QE—during what I consider a period of fairly strong growth, fairly stable financial markets, fairly stable prices—we started doing it for various seasons, for various reasons, and by doing so we raised the bar for the next crisis. Because no matter what you did before, it could never be enough. July 13, 2025, Fox News Interview: The Fed's policy mix is ​​completely wrong—it has a huge balance sheet, like we're in the 2008 crisis or the 2020 pandemic, and interest rates are too high. It needs to shrink the Fed's balance sheet and lower interest rates. That way, ordinary streets can get lower borrowing costs. July 17, 2025, CNBC Squawk Box Interview: We need regime change in policy implementation. In my view, the credibility deficit lies with the current Fed administration. I think their hesitation to cut interest rates is actually a pretty bad sign for them. The specter of their mistakes on inflation keeps haunting them. I think a change of regime at the Federal Reserve will happen in due time. Regarding the Treasury-Fed agreement: We need a new Treasury-Fed agreement, just like we did in 1951, after we had accumulated national debt and the central bank's and Treasury's objectives were at odds. That's where things are now. If we reach a new agreement, then the Federal Reserve Chairman and the Treasury Secretary can clearly and prudently describe to the market, "This is our target for the size of the Federal Reserve's balance sheet." A smaller balance sheet has many advantages, including better economic outcomes. We have two policy tools, imperfect substitutes for each other, sometimes contradictory, sometimes working together. But if the printing presses can be quieted, you can have low policy rates. "The Federal Reserve's Broken" (November 2025 Wall Street Journal column) Inflation is a choice, and the record of the Federal Reserve under Chairman Powell is a record of unwise choices. If the Fed picks up its game, Americans will have higher wages and greater purchasing power. AI will be a significant anti-inflationary force, boosting productivity and enhancing American competitiveness. Productivity gains should drive significant growth in real after-tax wages. A one percentage point increase in annual productivity growth will double the standard of living within a generation. The Fed should abandon the dogma that inflation is caused by too fast economic growth and too high worker wages. The Fed's bloated balance sheet—designed to support the largest companies of past crisis eras—can be significantly reduced. This generosity can be redeployed in the form of lower interest rates to support households and small and medium-sized enterprises. The cost of curiosity is approaching zero due to a new era of innovation in the United States, while the rewards for curiosity are soaring, largely thanks to the pro-growth policies championed by President Trump.

data.title: In 2023, Jeff Yan discussed high-frequency computing and why Hyperliquid was developed.
data.description:
This episode features Jeff Yan, founder of Hyperliquid. Jeff began his career in the high-frequency trading (HF) space at Hudson River before moving into the crypto world, where he built one of the largest market makers in the sector. He delves into the infrastructure of centralized crypto exchanges, adversarial algorithms, and why HFT (high-frequency trading) profits and losses can actually be predictive of medium-term price movements. He explains what he sees as the problems with current decentralized exchanges and introduces the early ideas behind Hyperliquid. This episode, released on May 8, 2023, offers a glimpse into many of Jeff Yan's early thoughts. How to Get into Crypto Trading from Harvard

Jeff Yan: My experience is similar to many HFT practitioners: I graduated from Harvard University with a major in Computer Science and Mathematics, and then directly joined Hudson River Trading, a large market-making institution in traditional finance. I traded US stocks at the time, and the experience was excellent. When I joined, the company had about 150 people, and now it's much larger. I benefited greatly from this experience, being exposed to the most interesting problems. Engineering and mathematics can be perfectly combined, which is almost "heaven" for quantitative trading. In 2018, with the rise of Ethereum smart contracts, I read the (Ethereum) Yellow Paper and instantly "got it." I was convinced that this was the future, so I left to work on an L2-oriented exchange protocol. We chose to enter the prediction market sector because Augur was already showing strong product-market fit (PMF), and we were more skilled in and focused on the underlying technical capabilities of exchanges. Therefore, after securing funding, we moved to San Francisco to build our team. However, a few months later, I decided to shut down the project because the timing wasn't right: regulatory uncertainty was extremely high, and user acquisition was very difficult. At that time, most people weren't familiar with smart contracts and were more interested in token speculation; the real demand for DeFi hadn't yet materialized, so the project was ultimately shelved. I then spent some time reflecting and traveling before finally returning to trading. Compared to constantly searching for PMF in the market, trading itself was more direct and interesting. Initially, I considered joining an established company, but considering my experience with crypto products and my familiarity with industry mechanisms, I started with proprietary crypto trading. Initially, this was just a side business, but I quickly saw significant opportunities, and the business expanded much faster than expected. The market's inefficiency surprised me. I then dedicated myself to it for nearly three years: a truly systematic launch occurred in early 2020, coinciding perfectly with the market cycle. As the market size and trading volume grew tenfold or even a hundredfold, we grew in tandem, eventually reaching the top tier of centralized exchange (CEX) market makers. About a year ago, we began systematically evaluating DeFi trading opportunities. This was similar to our early observations when entering CEX trading—inefficiencies were widespread. However, the difference was that some DeFi protocols had inherent flaws in their mechanism design, limiting the trading experience and capital efficiency. Simultaneously, after the FTX incident, the market's awareness of "not your keys, not your coins" and counterparty risk significantly strengthened, leading to a continued rise in demand for truly decentralized products. Based on the above changes, we believe the window of opportunity for building decentralized exchanges has arrived. Over the past one to two quarters, we have continuously invested resources in advancing this direction; our high-frequency trading (HFT) business is operating and maintaining relatively stably, while our current main investment and focus is on solidly implementing and systematically building this decentralized trading technology stack. Market Making vs. Order Taking: What's the Difference? Jeff Yan: In my view, this is indeed the first major decision to make when entering high-frequency trading. From a macro perspective, the two have many similarities: both inherently have extremely high infrastructure requirements and are highly sensitive to latency. However, in many key aspects, they exhibit opposite emphases: market making relies more on infrastructure capabilities; order taking relies more on statistical and mathematical modeling. I believe the choice of which path to take depends primarily on the type of work and research you prefer to invest in. Taking market making as an example, you are to some extent constrained by counterparties who "push through" the price, leaving very little room for error. Typically, using leverage and placing orders across multiple instruments and price levels creates significant implicit risk exposure; once an error occurs, the cost of tail risk is often very high. In contrast, a taker strategy can be triggered only once a day—and can still be an effective high-frequency strategy, possibly based on news or a specific type of signal. Because of the fewer triggers, you have the space to refine your model: not triggering most of the time doesn't matter, as long as the performance is good enough when it does. Conversely, market making lacks this flexibility—even if it operates well 99% of the time, a mere 1% lag or failure to keep up with the data can wipe out the entire PnL for the remaining 99%. This is the fundamental difference between "infrastructure-driven" and "model-driven" approaches. Corey Hoffstein: Could it be understood more intuitively like this: the party choosing to "take orders" is willing to cross the bid-ask spread because they expect the price to continue moving in their direction, thus willing to bear the spread cost; while the party "making markets" hopes the price will remain as stable as possible within their trading window—when someone crosses the spread to trade with them, they then hedge or reverse the trade on the other side to profit from the spread. Is this distinction reasonable? That is, one party prefers a sideways market within their trading window, while the other prefers directional movement. Jeff Yan: Yes, that's basically how it works. In high-frequency trading, we usually evaluate markout (look-back profits) using extremely short time windows, but this judgment also holds true for more general trading frequencies: as long as you choose to "take a bite," at the moment measured at the midpoint, you will immediately incur a certain loss (spread and fees). Only if, subsequently, within your set prediction time window, the average price movement can cover this immediate loss and further compensate for the fees, will your strategy have a positive expected value. Market making is exactly the opposite: at the moment of execution, your "initial PnL" is often at the highest level that the trade can achieve—because you have just captured a spread. What you are betting on is that this portion of the profit, on average, will not be completely eroded by "deteriorating selection." Therefore, in a market scenario, if all transactions are observed using markout over time, a decrease in PnL over time is usually the more common pattern; your expectation is simply that the decrease should not become negative. Corey Hoffstein: Before our call, you mentioned that the most difficult part of scaling up a business is not research, but infrastructure. I also saw a similar statement on X: "Being able to do data normalization doesn't guarantee you'll make money, but not being able to will definitely prevent you from making money." Could you talk about your biggest lessons learned regarding infrastructure, and why it's so crucial? Jeff Yan: This question can be roughly divided into two parts, and the two are closely related: one is "transaction infrastructure," and the other is "research infrastructure." Data cleaning leans more towards the latter, falling under the umbrella of statistical practice; the former refers to high-frequency trading systems in a narrower sense. Both are extremely important.
